dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for heavy vaginal bleeding and possible hemorrhage.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having heavy vaginal bleeding for the last four days - it hasn't stopped.
Thank you for sharing. Have you noticed any other symptoms or issues?
Yes, I've been feeling weak and a bit dizzy from all the bleeding.
Can you tell me your name?
Mary.
And where are we now?
In my house.
And what day is this?
I don't know the date exactly, but it's the morning.
I'm now going to check your airway for any signs of obstruction, so I'll gently clear my throat for observation.
Your breathing seems to be steady and unlabored right now.
Your respirations are approximately 21 per minute and of normal depth.
I'm hearing equal breath sounds on both sides.
Pulse oximetry is displaying a reading of 100% oxygen saturation.
I'm going to assess your circulation next.
Your pulse is rapid, at 136 beats per minute, and it feels weak.
There is pallor consistent with possible blood loss, and we note the vital finding of low blood pressure.
We're feeling pulses in your arms and legs, though they are very faint.
Pulse is 136 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 21 per minute.
Blood pressure is 85 over 64.
We attempted to take a glucose level, but we didn't get a reading.
Oxygen saturation remains at 100 percent.
The four lead ECG shows a rhythm of sinus tachycardia.
I will now attach the cardiac monitor for ongoing heart rhythm assessment.
I'm placing the pads now.
The ECG monitor is now showing stable sinus tachycardia.
I must start an IV in your arm in order to give you fluids.
I'm rolling up your sleeve now and selecting a good vein.
IV has been placed successfully in an extremity vein and I'm administering 250 mL of normal saline.
Your pulse now reads 124 and is showing some improvement.
Respiratory rate has increased to 25.
Blood pressure is now 96 over 69.
Oxygen saturation has decreased to 85%.
The EKG is again showing sinus tachycardia with no new changes.
I will now check your vitals again for any further developments.
Let's now observe and assess for further signs of bleeding or hemodynamic changes.
I understand you had a brief episode of losing consciousness before we arrived. Can you tell me about something you read recently or something you did yesterday?
There is no rigidity or neck trauma that I can see at this time.
I'm assessing arms and legs for any injury or abnormal movement.
You're moving all your limbs as we go through the exam.
I am able to feel pulses in both lower extremities and both arms.
Do you feel any pain in any part of your body besides your abdomen?
How would you rate your pain on a scale from 1 to 10?
It's a 2, but I still feel weak and unsteady.
Have there been any episodes of losing consciousness?
Yes, around 40 seconds before you arrived.
Can you tell me when the bleeding started and any symptoms you've had while it was ongoing?
It started four days ago and I've been feeling weak and dizzy.
Are you allergic to any medications or have any contraindications?
I don't have any known allergies to medications.
What medications do you take on a regular basis?
I take Tranexamic Acid and Tri-Sprintec.
What is your medical history?
I have had seizures due to epilepsy.
When did you last have food or drink?
It was the day before this all started, I think.
What caused you to call 911?
Because I kept bleeding and couldn't stop it at home.
Given the situation, we will transport you to VCU Medical Center for further treatment.
That sounds good to me. I just need to be moved carefully.
We'll use a two-person technique to lift you from your bed for your safety.
You'll be placed on a stairchair for assistance in moving downstairs.
May I have your name and age?
I'm Mary; I am 30 years old.
Mary is 30 years old, with a self-reported clotting disorder.
She is currently on Tranexamic Acid and Tri-Sprintec and has a history of seizures from epilepsy.
She has four days of ongoing, non-stop vaginal bleeding. Her pulse is 136, BP 85/64 with 85% oxygen saturation.
She was observed to lose consciousness for around 40 seconds before our arrival and will be transported in a semi-Fowler's position.
We have placed an IV in an extremity vein and given 250 mL of normal saline. An initial EKG showed sinus tachycardia and a glucose level could not be read.
She currently has a pain level of 2, reports no acute distress, but oxygen saturation has dropped to 85%.
Her most recent monitored vital signs are 137 pulse, 90/52 BP, 22 respirations, and 77% oxygen saturation.
She has no allergies reported and arrived at the scene without signs of trauma or rigidity, but was reporting dizziness and weakness.
Call type was Hemorrhage/Lacerations; no visible lacerations, but heavy vaginal hemorrhage noted during assessment.
All medical interventions as per protocol 4-14 were completed: IV placed, 250 mL fluids administered, and EKG applied.
Transport equipment and all patient documentation are now being compiled and will be transferred at the hospital.
Mary, we're now reaching for the stairchair to assist in moving you to the stretcher.
Two of us will manage this transfer to ensure it is done safely and carefully.
You will now be placed in the semi-Fowler's position for the ride to the hospital.
Transport is now ready to begin and all transferred equipment will accompany us.
Medical updates have been transmitted to the receiving facility.
A stairchair will be used to assist with transport to the stretcher without physical complications.
Our call ahead to VCU Medical Center has confirmed arrival and all critical parameters will be reviewed on transfer.
Transport details and patient condition are being maintained throughout the ride.
All patient updates, from IV fluids to vital readings, are being tracked and documented per EPCR guidelines.
Mary has been safely moved using the stairchair and is now in position for ambulance transfer.
The receiving hospital is informed of pre-arrival medical status and has been prepared for Mary's condition.
All necessary medications, vitals, and interventions have been covered for hospital continuity of care.
Our team is monitoring vitals during transport and we remain close for en route emergencies.
The IV, EKG, and vital updates will be transferred for continued hospital monitoring and intervention.
All handoff notes have been transmitted and our documentation is complete for EPCR compliance.
EMT treatment and documentation were followed according to field medical directives and protocol.
The stretcher is now entering the ambulance as we complete the transfer.
The receiving nurse has been informed and the ambulance travel time remains within 1 hour.
Our team remains prepared for questions from the hospital and full handoff is ready.
Transportation to VCU Medical Center with Mary in a stable position is currently underway.
All patient care during transport is being recorded and will be handed over for continued hospital care.
The receiving team has been notified and is prepared to accept Mary upon our arrival to the hospital.
The condition deteriorates slowly, so diligent monitoring remains imperative.
Documents and electronic records will be delivered to the nursing staff for their immediate use.
All procedures were conducted with the patient's cooperation and each step was done according to protocol.
All nursing care directives have been communicated to the hospital for immediate intervention if needed.
EKG and IV monitoring are to remain in place through the hospital's resumption of care.
The patient has arrived at VCU Medical Center and is being turned over to the receiving team now.
Mary is stable and ready for handoff at VCU Medical Center.
Our EPCR documentation is complete and all patient-related events and treatments have been recorded.
Final documentation is being prepared for timely submission in compliance with medical record keeping.
All pre-hospital interventions and transfer protocols have been fulfilled and reported.
This patient handoff is complete with no complications, and the receiving RN is now taking over care.
Continuity of care has been ensured for the receiving team with complete handoff notes and treatments as necessary.
